statement: [arXiv:pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f Main Theorem (contractibility of arc and curve-system complexes)] Let S be a compact connected surface, possibly with boundary, and let V be a nonempty finite subset of S. An essential arc in (S,V) means an embedded arc meeting ∂S ∪ V only in its endpoints, whose endpoints lie in V and may coincide, and which does not separate S into two components one of which is a disk meeting V only in the endpoints of the arc. An essential circle in (S,V) means an embedded circle in S disjoint from ∂S ∪ V which does not separate S into two components one of which is either a disk meeting V in at most one point or an annulus disjoint from V. Let A(S,V) be the simplicial complex whose k-simplices are ambient-isotopy classes rel V of systems of k+1 pairwise non-isotopic essential arcs, disjoint except possibly at endpoints. Let C(S,V) be the analogous complex of systems of pairwise non-isotopic essential arcs and essential circles, mutually disjoint except possibly for arc endpoints. Excluding the trivial cases in which the complex under discussion is empty: (a) A(S,V) is contractible except when S is a disk with V contained in ∂S or when S is an annulus with V contained in one component of ∂S; in these exceptional cases A(S,V) is homeomorphic to a sphere. (b) If V contains at least one point in the interior of S, then C(S,V) is contractible.
proof_sketch: First prove (a) when V has at most one point on each boundary component. Choose an essential arc β. The surgery construction stated separately below gives a deformation retraction of A(S,V) onto the closed star of [β], the subcomplex of arc systems disjoint from β; a star is contractible. For general V, use the proposition that if V′ is obtained from V by adding one new vertex on a boundary component already containing a vertex, then A(S,V′) is homeomorphic to the suspension ΣA(S,V). The proof of this proposition compares both spaces by decomposing A(S,V′) into the subcomplex X of systems not containing either of two boundary-parallel arcs β,β′ adjacent to the new vertex, plus the two stars St(β), St(β′). Collapsing the boundary segment between the old and new vertex sends X into ΣA(S,V); the suspension coordinate is (θ−θ′)/2, where θ and θ′ are the total weights of arcs incident to the two adjacent vertices. The two complementary open cones are precisely the stars of β and β′, giving a PL homeomorphism. Inductively remove extra boundary vertices until each boundary component has at most one vertex. Suspension preserves contractibility, while repeated suspension of the initial S^0 cases gives spheres. The initial S^0 exceptional cases are the quadrilateral disk and the annulus with two vertices on one boundary component, yielding exactly the stated disk and annulus exceptions after adding boundary vertices. For (b), if V has an interior point, choose an essential arc β with both endpoints at that interior vertex; the same surgery deformation works for curve systems containing circles as well as arcs and retracts C(S,V) to St([β]), hence C(S,V) is contractible.

statement: [arXiv:pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f Surgery deformation to the star of a fixed arc (construction in the proof of the Main Theorem)] Let S be a compact connected surface and V a nonempty finite subset. Let β be an essential arc in (S,V). In the arc-only case, assume V has at most one point in each component of ∂S and set X = A(S,V). In the curve-system case, assume β has both endpoints at a point of V lying in the interior of S and set X = C(S,V). Then X admits a deformation retraction, indeed a flow which is linear on simplices in the sense described below, onto the closed star St_X([β]). This star consists of all simplices represented by curve systems disjoint from β, allowing one member of the system to be isotopic to β. The deformation fixes St_X([β]) pointwise.
proof_sketch: For a simplex [α0,...,αk], isotope the representing system c1 = α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk into minimal position with β: there is no disk whose boundary is an arc in β and an arc in some αi, not equal to αi ∪ β, with interior disjoint from V. The construction is independent of the chosen minimal representative because any two minimal positions are isotopic through minimal positions: take an isotopy F from one to the other, perturb F transverse to β, then simplify the 1-manifold F^{-1}(β) by cancelling critical points of its projection to the isotopy parameter. View a point P = Σ ti αi of the simplex as a weighted curve system by replacing αi with parallel copies of total thickness ti. At the crossings with β, pinch these families into one band of total thickness θ. Choose an end of β. For t ∈ [0,1], cut into this band from the chosen end through thickness tθ and redirect the two cut ends along β to that end. Discard arcs which become inessential, in particular boundary-parallel arcs as in the paper's Figure 3; in the arc-only case, the hypothesis of at most one vertex on each boundary component ensures that not all of the weighted system is discarded. Equivalently and more combinatorially, order the intersection points x1,...,xm of c1 with β from the chosen end. Slide x1 to the end of β, then discard inessential or parallel pieces, obtaining a new curve system c2 meeting β in x2,...,xm. The union c1 ∪ c2 is again a curve system and determines a simplex σ1 containing the original simplex as a face; the initial part of the deformation moves linearly along fibers of the linear projection of σ1 to the face corresponding to c2. Repeat, sliding x2, then x3, and so on, producing c3,...,cm+1 with cm+1 disjoint from β. Thus each starting simplex is crossed by a finite sequence of linear motions through simplices σ1,...,σm. The endpoint lies in St([β]). If the starting system is already disjoint from β, there are no intersections and the flow is stationary. The construction on a face is exactly the restriction of the construction on the containing simplex, since it uses the same ordered intersections with weights of omitted curves set to zero. Hence the simplexwise flows glue to a continuous global deformation retraction.

statement: [arXiv:pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f Cutting-open/link identification (used in the proof of the Corollary on elementary moves)] Let τ = [α0,...,αk] be a simplex of A(S,V), represented by a system of essential arcs in a compact connected surface S with finite vertex set V. Let (S_τ,V_τ) be the possibly disconnected surface with vertices obtained by cutting S open along representatives of the arcs α0,...,αk; for disconnected surfaces, define A(S_τ,V_τ) to be the join of the arc complexes of the connected components. Then the link of τ in A(S,V) is naturally identified with A(S_τ,V_τ). Consequently, using the Main Theorem, whenever this cut-open arc complex has dimension at least one it is connected; in particular, in the corollary's application, links of simplices of codimension at least two are connected.
proof_sketch: A simplex in the link of τ is represented by an arc system γ disjoint from α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk and containing no arc isotopic rel V to any αi. Cutting S along the αi turns γ into an arc system in S_τ. Conversely, any essential arc system in S_τ glues to an arc system in S disjoint from the αi. The essentialness conditions match: an arc in the cut surface that cut off a disk with only its endpoints as vertices would glue to an inessential arc in S, while arcs parallel to the new boundary pieces created by cutting correspond precisely to arcs isotopic to one of the αi and hence are excluded from the link. Ambient isotopies rel vertices disjoint from the αi lift to isotopies in S_τ, and isotopies in S_τ descend after gluing, so the correspondence is on isotopy classes and respects disjoint unions of arcs; therefore it is a simplicial isomorphism. If S_τ has several components, an arc system is exactly a union of independent systems in the components, which is why the complex is the join of the component complexes. For connectivity, the Main Theorem implies each nonempty component arc complex is either contractible or a sphere; any such complex of positive dimension is connected, and joins are connected as soon as the total dimension is at least one. This link-connectivity is what allows a polygonal path between maximal simplices to be pushed off simplices of codimension greater than one.

statement: [arXiv:2212.03949 Theorem 4.11] Let P be a finite bounded poset with minimum hat0 and maximum hat1. A rooted interval [u,v]_r means the interval [u,v] together with a fixed saturated chain r from hat0 to u. A chain-atom ordering assigns, for every rooted interval [u,hat1]_r, a total order on the atoms of that rooted interval, i.e. on the elements covering u; its restriction to [u,v]_r is the induced order on atoms lying below v. A recursive atom ordering (RAO), applied recursively to rooted intervals, is an atom order a1,...,at of a finite bounded rooted interval [u,v]_r such that: (R1a) for every j, [a_j,v]_{r∪a_j} has an RAO; (R1b) for j>1, in the chosen RAO of [a_j,v]_{r∪a_j}, all atoms x covering a_j that are above some earlier atom a_i with i<j precede all atoms not above any earlier atom; and (R2) for all i<j and all y with a_i<y and a_j<y, there exist k<j and z such that a_j ⋖ z, a_k<z, and z≤y. A generalized recursive atom ordering (GRAO) is defined the same way except that (R1b) is replaced by: (G1b) for every j and every x,w with a_j ⋖ x ⋖ w≤v, when the chosen GRAO of [a_j,v] is restricted to [a_j,w], either the first atom of [a_j,w] is above some earlier atom a_i with i<j, or no atom of [a_j,w] is above any earlier atom. Then P admits a GRAO if and only if P admits an RAO. More precisely, every RAO is a GRAO. Conversely, every GRAO can be transformed into an RAO by the atom reordering process: starting with a chain-atom ordering Λ, choose any linear extension of P; leave the atom order at hat0 unchanged; then, proceeding upward through the linear extension and through all roots r for [u,hat1], let u^- be the element immediately below u in r and r^- the root with u deleted. Using the already reordered atom order in [u^-,hat1]_{r^-}, put first all atoms x of [u,hat1]_r that are above some atom of [u^-,hat1]_{r^-} preceding u, in their original relative Λ-order, and then all remaining atoms, again in their original relative Λ-order. If Λ is a GRAO, the resulting chain-atom ordering Λ^re is an RAO.
proof_sketch: RAO ⇒ GRAO is by induction on longest chain length. Conditions (R1a) and (R2) are exactly the recursive and exchange conditions needed for GRAO. For (G1b), use that an RAO gives a CL-labeling and CL-labelings restrict to intervals; converting the restricted CL-labeling back to an RAO shows the RAO restricted to [a_j,w] still has the all earlier-overlap atoms first property, which implies the weaker GRAO alternative. For GRAO ⇒ RAO the proof builds a controlled reordering. Key preliminary facts: (1) a GRAO restricts to every rooted subinterval [u,v]_r; (2) the GRAO alternative extends from covers to arbitrary [a_j,v]: either the first atom is above an earlier atom or no atom is; (3) if two consecutive atoms a_i,a_{i+1} in [u,hat1]_r are never the first atom of any rooted interval [u,w]_r containing both, then swapping them preserves the GRAO. The swap lemma is the technical core: one proves the F and G sets of atoms above earlier atoms are unchanged by such a swap, then checks separately the recursive condition, the GRAO first-atom alternative, and the exchange condition. Next, the atom reordering process is shown to preserve the first atom of every rooted interval [u,v]_r. This is proved by induction on the root length. If the first atom x of [u,v]_r were moved behind another atom, then x lies in the G part. If x is above an earlier atom below u, the earliest atom in the lower interval [u^-,x] would have to move after u, contradicting induction. If no atom of [u,v]_r is above an earlier atom, then u was already first in [u^-,v] and remains first by induction, so every atom of [u,v]_r lies in G and x cannot move. Since the reordering preserves first atoms, each permutation it performs on atoms of [u,hat1]_r can be written as adjacent transpositions each preserving first atoms in all subintervals; the swap lemma implies the entire reordered chain-atom ordering remains a GRAO. Finally induct on longest chain length. Below each atom a_j, the reordered order is obtained from a GRAO on [a_j,hat1] by a first-atom-preserving initial permutation and then by atom reordering, hence is an RAO by induction. The construction itself enforces RAO condition (R1b), and because the output is still a GRAO it satisfies the common exchange condition (R2). Thus Λ^re is an RAO.

statement: [arXiv:2212.03949 Theorem 6.4] Let P be a finite bounded poset. A chain-edge labeling labels each cover relation x⋖y with a value that may depend on the root, i.e. on the saturated chain from hat0 to x, with the usual consistency that identical initial rooted chains give identical labels on their common edges. For a rooted interval [u,v]_r, label sequences of saturated chains are compared lexicographically. A topological ascent at x⋖y⋖z means that the two-edge label sequence through y is lexicographically earliest among all saturated chains from x to z with the same root; otherwise it is a topological descent. A CL-labeling is a chain-edge labeling for which every rooted interval has a unique strictly ascending saturated chain and that chain has lexicographically first label sequence. A CC-labeling is a chain-edge labeling for which every rooted interval has a unique saturated chain consisting entirely of topological ascents, and in each rooted interval all saturated chains have distinct label sequences with no label sequence a prefix of another. A TCL-labeling is a chain-edge labeling for which every rooted interval has a unique lexicographically earliest saturated chain and every other saturated chain has at least one topological descent. The unique earliest, or UE, property means that in every rooted interval [u,v]_r the smallest label occurring on an initial cover u⋖a, with a an atom of [u,v], occurs for exactly one such atom. A labeling with unique lexicographically earliest chains is self-consistent if whenever a is the atom on the lexicographically first saturated chain of [u,v]_r and b≠a is another atom of [u,v]_r, then for every v' with a,b≤v', every saturated chain of [u,v']_r through a is lexicographically earlier than every saturated chain of [u,v']_r through b. With RAO and GRAO as in Theorem 4.11, the following eight conditions on P are equivalent: (1) P admits an RAO; (2) P admits a GRAO; (3) P admits a CL-labeling; (4) P admits a CL-labeling with the UE property; (5) P admits a self-consistent CC-labeling; (6) P admits a CC-labeling with the UE property; (7) P admits a self-consistent TCL-labeling; (8) P admits a TCL-labeling with the UE property. Moreover, each implication is constructive: either the object satisfying one condition is already an object satisfying another, or the proof gives an explicit construction of the latter from the former.
proof_sketch: The proof is a web of constructive implications. (1)⇔(2) is Theorem 4.11: atom reordering turns any GRAO into an RAO, and every RAO is a GRAO. (1)⇔(3) is Björner-Wachs: finite bounded posets admitting RAOs are exactly CL-shellable posets. (3)⇔(4) uses Lemma 5.5, which proves every CL-labeling has UE. The key contradiction is: if two atoms a,a' of [u,v]_r share the minimum first label, take the lex-first chain M through a and the lex-first chain M' through a'. Since M is ascending, λ(u,a)< its next label; since M is lexicographically before M' and first labels agree, the next label on M is ≤ the next label on M'. Hence M' also ascends at a'. M' is ascending above a' because it is lex-first in [a',v], so [u,v]_r has two ascending chains, impossible. UE implies self-consistency (Lemma 5.3), because the unique atom with smallest first label beats every chain through every other atom in all larger intervals with the same root. RAOs also directly give CC-labelings with UE by labeling u⋖a_i with the index i in the recursively chosen atom order; distinct index sequences and the RAO exchange axiom force every non-first chain to have a topological descent. Conversely, a self-consistent TCL-labeling gives a GRAO (Theorem 6.3). Construct the atom order at [u,hat1]_r greedily: choose a1 from a lexicographically earliest saturated chain; after choosing a1,...,ai, choose a_{i+1} from a lexicographically earliest chain avoiding those atoms. Self-consistency makes this order compatible with lex-first choices in all subintervals. Inductively the upper intervals have GRAOs. For GRAO condition (G1b), suppose some atom of [a_j,w] is above an earlier atom but the first atom x1 of [a_j,w] is not. The lex-first chain through x1 then combines with the topological ascent hat0⋖a_j⋖x1 to give one topologically ascending chain to w, while the earlier atom below w gives another, contradicting uniqueness. For the exchange condition, if a_i<a_j both lie below y with i<j, the lex-first chain in [a_j,y] cannot remain topologically ascending from hat0, because the lex-first chain through the earlier atom already gives the unique topologically ascending chain; therefore there is a topological descent at the bottom, and the alternative two-step path supplies z and an earlier atom a_k<z with a_j⋖z≤y. CC-labelings are TCL-labelings because distinct non-prefix label sequences and uniqueness of the topologically ascending chain imply uniqueness of the lex-first chain and descents on all others. These implications give all eight equivalences, including the UE variants.

statement: [arXiv:2212.03949 Theorem 7.1] For every n, let P_n be the uncrossing poset of complete matchings on 2n cyclically ordered boundary nodes, with an added minimum element hat0. The elements other than hat0 are perfect matchings, represented by strand diagrams. A noncrossing matching covers hat0. More generally, a matching u is covered by a matching v when v has rank one larger than u, where the rank is one plus the minimal possible number of strand crossings, and u is obtained from v by uncrossing a single crossing pair of strands in one of the two possible ways. The order is generated by these cover relations. Then P_n is dual CL-shellable; equivalently, the dual poset P_n^* admits a CL-labeling.
proof_sketch: Use the dual EC-labeling of Hersh-Kenyon. Encode a matching by numbering strands 1,...,n in the order their first endpoints are encountered clockwise from a fixed base boundary node, then reading the endpoint strand numbers clockwise; this gives a word of length 2n with two copies of each number and first occurrences increasing. If i<j and a crossing pair in v has subsequence i j i j, the two uncrossings replace it by either i j j i or i i j j. For a cover u⋖v in P_n, label the downward step v→u by (i,j) in the first case i j i j → i j j i, and by (j,i) in the second case i j i j → i i j j. Covers from noncrossing matchings down to hat0 receive a special label L. Order labels by (i,j)<L<(j,i) for i<j; among labels (i,j) with i<j use lexicographic order; among labels (j,i) with i<j use reverse lexicographic order. Hersh-Kenyon prove this is a dual EC-labeling of P_n. To upgrade to dual CL-shellability, apply the paper's Corollary 6.5, a consequence of Theorem 6.4: any self-consistent EC-labeling can be algorithmically converted to a CL-labeling. It remains to prove self-consistency, and the paper does so via the UE property. Fix an element v of P_n. The labels on all cover relations downward from v are distinct: a label K determines the two strand numbers being uncrossed, and its order, (i,j) versus (j,i), determines which of the two uncrossings was performed; this data uniquely determines the lower matching u. The special label L occurs only for the unique downward cover from a noncrossing matching to hat0. Hence, in the dual poset, all labels on covers upward from any fixed element are distinct, so in particular the smallest such label occurs uniquely. Thus the dual EC-labeling has the UE property. Lemma 5.3 gives UE ⇒ self-consistent, and Corollary 6.5 then yields a dual CL-labeling.

statement: [arXiv:1803.06217 Theorem 3.18 and Corollary 3.19] Fix n ≥ 2. Let P_n be the uncrossing poset whose nonzero elements are pairings of 2n cyclically ordered boundary points by n wires, with the fully crossed pairing as maximum and with an adjoined minimum 0-hat; a diagram D′ is covered by D in P_n when D′ is obtained from D by locally uncrossing one crossing pair of wires in one of the two possible ways and the minimal crossing number drops by exactly one, equivalently no pair of wires becomes double-crossed. Let P_n^* be the dual. Choose a boundary basepoint and, for every diagram D, label endpoints clockwise as follows: the first endpoint of the i-th new wire encountered is i_p and its second endpoint is i_v; let w(D) be the word with the p/v subscripts suppressed. Define an edge-labeling λ of covers D ≺ D′ in P_n^* as follows. If the cover uncrosses crossing wires k < m whose induced subword in w(D) is k,m,k,m, then the resolution whose induced subword in w(D′) is k,m,m,k has label (k,m), while the resolution whose induced subword in w(D′) is k,k,m,m has label (m,k). Covers from a noncrossing diagram to the artificial top of P_n^* are labeled L. Order labels by (i,j) with i < j lexicographically, then L, then labels (r,s) with r > s ordered by decreasing second coordinate and, for equal second coordinate, decreasing first coordinate: (n,n−1) < (n,n−2) < (n−1,n−2) < ... < (2,1). With this labeling, every interval of P_n^* has a unique saturated chain all of whose length-two subchains are lexicographically first among the saturated chains in the corresponding rank-two interval; equivalently λ is an EC-labeling of P_n^*. Hence P_n^* is EC-shellable and P_n is shellable. Moreover, since Lam proved P_n is Eulerian, P_n is a CW poset: for every x > 0-hat in P_n, the order complex of the open interval (0-hat,x) is homeomorphic to a sphere of dimension rank(x)−2, so P_n is the face poset of a regular CW complex with 0-hat representing the empty cell.
proof_sketch: The proof is by analyzing intervals of P_n^*. For intervals [u,v] with v below the artificial top, Lemma 4.1 shows that every ordinary label descent is already a topological descent, so a topologically ascending chain must have weakly increasing labels. If the start set S(D), the set of positions of p-endpoints in w(D), is unchanged across the interval, the map D ↦ π(D), where π(D) is the permutation obtained by reading the v-endpoints, identifies the interval with a type A Bruhat interval; under this identification λ is Dyer's reflection-order EL-labeling, so the weakly increasing chain is unique. If the start set changes, only labels (j,i) with j > i move p-endpoints. The imposed order on these labels forces the last such move: choose the smallest i whose v-end in the target either occupies a source start-position or contains nested below it a v-end occupying such a position; then the first coordinate j is determined by the word just before that position. If two different predecessors of v had the same forced label, the chain would require a lower label (t,i) and the local three-wire analysis of Proposition 4.3 would create a topological descent. Iterating determines the whole moving-start part, and the remaining fixed-start part is the Bruhat increasing chain. For intervals [u, artificial top], the lexicographically first chain is constructed greedily: choose the smallest wire i crossing some larger wire, then the smallest such j, and uncross to the resolution with subword i,j,j,i. A renaming argument shows this step cannot make a smaller label available, so the greedy label sequence is weakly increasing and ends with L. Any other saturated chain either deviates before the top, in which case the preceding interval analysis gives a topological descent, or uses a label (j,i) with j > i before the final L, which is itself a descent since (j,i) > L. Thus every interval has a unique topologically ascending chain. EC-shellability gives shellability. Finally, Lam's Eulerian theorem implies thinness, and the Björner-Danaraj-Klee criterion says a finite graded thin shellable poset with a minimum is a CW poset.

statement: [arXiv:1803.06217 Lemma 3.16] Let D be a wire diagram in P_n with at least one crossing. For i < j, define the noncrossing-pair set N(D) as follows: include (i,j) in N(D) if the induced subword of w(D) on wires i and j is i,j,j,i, and include (j,i) in N(D) if that induced subword is i,i,j,j. Thus neither ordered pair is in N(D) exactly when wires i and j cross, i.e. the induced subword is i,j,i,j. Consider a candidate downward cover D′ ≺ D in P_n obtained by uncrossing a crossing pair of wires in the way whose dual P_n^* edge-label would be the ordered pair (k,m), with k ≠ m. This candidate is an actual cover relation D′ ≺ D in P_n if and only if (k,m) is not in N(D), (m,k) is not in N(D), and the following additional conditions hold: if k < m, then for every l with k < l < m one has exactly one of (k,l) and (l,m) in N(D), i.e. |{(k,l),(l,m)} ∩ N(D)| = 1; if k > m, then for every l with l < m or k < l one has exactly one of (k,l) and (l,m) in N(D), i.e. |{(k,l),(l,m)} ∩ N(D)| = 1.
proof_sketch: A downward step is a cover precisely when the chosen smoothing removes the crossing of wires k and m and does not introduce a double crossing; equivalently the minimal crossing number drops by exactly one. The ordered label (k,m) specifies which of the two smoothings is being tested. Pairs of wires not involving k or m are unchanged, and k,m themselves become noncrossing, so the only possible obstruction is a third wire l that becomes double-crossed with k or m. The cyclic order constraints from the endpoint naming greatly restrict which l can matter: for k < m only k < l < m can be dangerous; for k > m only l < m or k < l can be dangerous. For each such l one checks the allowable six-letter induced subwords on the three wires k,l,m. The smoothing labeled (k,m) is harmless exactly when, before smoothing, l is noncrossing in the specified orientation with exactly one of the two ordered pairs (k,l) and (l,m). If both ordered pairs are in N(D), or neither is, the local reconnection forces l to meet one of the other two wires twice, so the resulting diagram is not a cover. The two initial requirements (k,m),(m,k) not in N(D) say exactly that k and m really cross in D. This case check is the full no-double-crossing criterion, yielding the stated equivalence.

statement: [arXiv:1803.06217 Lemma 4.1] Equip P_n^* with the edge-labeling λ and label order described in Theorem 3.18: resolving crossing wires k < m to induced subword k,m,m,k has label (k,m), resolving them to k,k,m,m has label (m,k), covers to the artificial top have label L, with all (i,j), i < j, lexicographically below L and all (r,s), r > s, above L in decreasing-second-coordinate order. Let u < v < artificial top in P_n^*. If x ≺ y ≺ z is any length-two subchain occurring in any saturated chain from u to v and λ(x,y) > λ(y,z), then x ≺ y ≺ z is a topological descent: there exists another middle element y′ with x ≺ y′ ≺ z such that the label sequence (λ(x,y′), λ(y′,z)) is lexicographically smaller than (λ(x,y), λ(y,z)).
proof_sketch: It suffices to examine the two consecutive uncrossings from x to z. If they involve four distinct wires, the two uncrossings commute: performing them in the opposite order gives x ≺ y′ ≺ z. One then compares labels. Reversing the order preserves whether each individual label is of increasing type (a,b) with a < b or decreasing type (a,b) with a > b, because deleting unrelated wires does not change which endpoint of the two-wire pair is first. The paper checks the resulting cases from the label order: if both labels are decreasing, a descent forces the second coordinate of the first label to be smaller, making the swapped first label smaller; if the first is decreasing and the second increasing, either the increasing label is automatically smaller or its first coordinate is smaller; if both are increasing, a descent forces the second uncrossing's first coordinate to be smaller. In each case λ(x,y′) < λ(x,y). If the two uncrossings involve only three wires, restrict the diagram to those three wires, giving the corresponding rank-two situation in P_3^*. The relative order of relevant labels is preserved under this restriction and under reinclusion: increasing versus decreasing type is preserved, comparisons of minima are preserved, and ties are broken by the same comparison of maxima. A direct check of the labeled Hasse diagram of P_3^* shows every descent there has a lexicographically earlier alternate middle element. Lifting that alternate chain back to the original three wires inside P_n^* gives the required y′. The hypothesis z below the artificial top avoids the special L-labeled final cover, so all cases are covered.

statement: [arXiv:1501.03238 Proposition 2.7] Let A be a complementary alphabet, meaning a set equipped with a fixed-point-free involution B ↦ comp(B). Let P=p_1 p_2 ... p_{2n} be a word in A. For a rooted plane tree with n edges, label its half-edges 1,2,...,2n by walking counterclockwise around the tree from the root; write e(u,v) for the edge whose two half-edges have labels u and v. A plane tree is P-valid if for every edge e(u,v), the letters p_u and p_v are complementary. Fix indices i<j<i'<j'. The local moves are as follows: a type 1 move, when the adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') occur, replaces them by e(i,j') and e(j,i'); a type 2 move, when the adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') occur, replaces them by e(i,j) and e(i',j'). A local move on a P-valid tree is valid if the resulting tree is also P-valid. Then: (1) there is a valid type 1 local move on adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') if and only if there exists B in A such that p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). (2) there is a valid type 2 local move on adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') if and only if there exists B in A such that p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B).
proof_sketch: The combinatorial hypotheses, namely that the two relevant edges are adjacent and have indices i<j<i'<j', ensure that the stated replacement is a legal local move of plane trees. Validity is then exactly a four-letter condition. For type 1, the starting tree is P-valid, so p_i is complementary to p_j and p_{i'} is complementary to p_{j'}. The resulting tree is P-valid exactly when p_i is complementary to p_{j'} and p_j is complementary to p_{i'}. Let B=p_i. Since complements are unique and comp is an involution with no fixed points, the four complementary-pair requirements force p_j=comp(B), p_{j'}=comp(B), and p_{i'}=B. Conversely, if these equalities hold, then both original edges e(i,j), e(i',j') and both new edges e(i,j'), e(j,i') pair complementary letters, so the type 1 move is valid. The type 2 statement is the same argument with the initial and final pairs interchanged: initial validity of e(i,j') and e(j,i') and final validity of e(i,j), e(i',j') impose precisely the same equalities p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). The proposition is therefore an exact local label criterion for validity of the elementary move.

statement: [arXiv:1501.03238 Theorem 3.4] Let A be a complementary alphabet with complement map B ↦ comp(B), and let P=p_1 p_2 ... p_{2n} be a word in A. Run the following greedy stack algorithm from left to right. Store indexed letters on a stack. At step j, if the stack is nonempty and its top entry is an indexed letter (i,p_i) with p_i=comp(p_j), pop that top entry and record the ordered pair (i,j). Otherwise push (j,p_j). Let E(P) be the set of pairs recorded by the algorithm. Let V(P) denote the set of P-valid plane trees, equivalently noncrossing perfect matchings of {1,...,2n} in which matched positions carry complementary letters. If V(P) is nonempty, then the greedy algorithm terminates with an empty stack, and the recorded pairs E(P) form a P-valid plane tree T_0(P) in V(P).
proof_sketch: The main preliminary fact is Lemma 3.3, a block-removal property of the stack algorithm. If the algorithm pops p_i at step j, then while p_i sat on the stack all letters p_{i+1},...,p_{j-1} that were pushed above it must have been popped before step j. Hence the algorithm matches the whole interval {i,i+1,...,j} internally by a perfect matching, and deleting the contiguous block p_i...p_j from P leaves exactly the same stack evolution and recorded pairs outside the block. This is the LIFO/nesting mechanism that makes the greedy pairs noncrossing. To prove the theorem, assume for contradiction that Q=q_1...q_{2k} is a shortest word admitting at least one Q-valid plane tree but for which the greedy algorithm ends with a nonempty stack. A valid plane tree has a leaf edge e(s,s+1), so q_s and q_{s+1} are complementary; therefore the greedy algorithm must pop at least once. Let the first pop occur at step j+1; since there were no earlier pops, this pop removes q_j. By Lemma 3.3, deleting q_j q_{j+1} leaves a shorter word on which the greedy algorithm has the same final stack as it has on Q. If some Q-valid tree has the leaf e(j,j+1), removing that leaf gives a valid plane tree for the shorter word, contradicting minimality of Q. It remains only to ensure the existence of such a valid tree. Take any Q-valid tree. If it does not already contain e(j,j+1), the two consecutive half-edges j and j+1 occur in one of the few possible noncrossing configurations: both on left sides of adjacent edges, both on right sides of adjacent edges, or forming the peak configuration displayed in the paper. Since q_j and q_{j+1} are complementary, their partners have the complementary labels needed for Proposition 2.7, so an appropriate valid local move transforms the tree into one containing the leaf e(j,j+1). Deleting that leaf gives the desired shorter valid instance, contradiction. Therefore no minimal counterexample exists; whenever a valid plane tree exists, the greedy stack empties and its nested complementary pairs define a P-valid plane tree.

statement: [arXiv:1501.03238 Theorem 3.6 and Corollaries 3.8, 3.10] Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p_1...p_{2n} be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, and let T_0(P) be the P-valid plane tree produced by the greedy algorithm. Let G_P be the graph whose vertices are the P-valid plane trees and whose undirected edges join two trees related by a valid local move. Let G_P^+ be the directed graph with the same vertices, with a directed edge T -> T' exactly when T' is obtained from T by a valid local move of type 2. Then T_0(P) is the unique sink of G_P^+. Equivalently, T_0(P) has no outgoing valid type 2 move, and every P-valid plane tree T different from T_0(P) has at least one outgoing valid type 2 move. Moreover G_P^+ has no directed cycles, and from every P-valid plane tree T there is a directed path in G_P^+ consisting entirely of valid type 2 moves from T to T_0(P). In particular the underlying undirected graph G_P is connected.
proof_sketch: First show T_0 has no valid type 2 move. If T_0 contained adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') with i<j<i'<j' supporting a valid type 2 move, Proposition 2.7 would give p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). In the greedy construction of T_0, by the subtree/nesting property of plane trees, all material between i and j has already been matched internally when step j is reached, so p_i is on top of the stack. Since p_j is its complement, the greedy algorithm would pair i with j, producing e(i,j), contradiction. Next prove every T != T_0 has a valid type 2 move. Let j be the first half-edge at which T and T_0 differ in left/right status. If j were a left half-edge in T_0 and a right half-edge in T, say in edge e(i',j), then the interval i'+1,...,j-1 is a subtree of T; all its earlier half-edge statuses agree with T_0, hence the same nested matching occurs in T_0. Thus p_{i'} is on top of the greedy stack at step j and would be paired with p_j, forcing e(i',j) in T_0, contradiction. Therefore j is a right half-edge in T_0, paired there with some i<j, while i and j are both left half-edges in T. The interval i+1,...,j-1 is a subtree of T_0 and, by minimality of j, the same subtree occurs in T. Thus in T the edges beginning at i and j are adjacent in precisely the configuration of a type 2 move. Since T_0 pairs i with j, p_i and p_j are complementary; P-validity of T gives complementary partners on the two adjacent edges, and Proposition 2.7 makes the type 2 move valid. This proves uniqueness of the sink. For acyclicity, use the height function d_T=sum_v dist(v,v_0), where v_0 is the root. In the schematic for a type 2 move, the move decreases this total distance by at least one, and by more if the intervening subtree denoted C in the paper is nonempty; no type 2 move can preserve or increase d_T. Hence G_P^+ has no directed cycles. Since the graph is finite and every non-T_0 vertex has an outgoing edge, following any sequence of outgoing type 2 moves must eventually terminate, and the only possible terminal vertex is the unique sink T_0. Thus every valid plane tree has a directed type 2 path to T_0. The paper also proves connectedness of G_P independently by induction: two valid trees either share an edge, allowing induction on the inside and outside subtrees, or a valid local move can first be applied to one tree to create a shared edge.

statement: The region-label rule forbids lenses and interior cycles; hence any valid cell is represented by an ordinary chord diagram in which crossings are determined by endpoint interleaving.
